summary: I grew up in Eureka, Utah, a silver-lead-zinc mining town that sparked my early fascination with geology and mining. The community and my family there really encouraged my interest in the mining industry. This background laid the foundation for my later studies and career in geology.

entity: Harvard Graduate School of Arts and Sciences
summary: I attended the Harvard Graduate School of Arts and Sciences where I studied geology under Professor Hugh McKinstry. During my time there, I completed a Ph.D. thesis focused on using manganese oxide as a guide to ore in the Tintic District, Utah. Harvard was instrumental in shaping my academic and research skills.

Professor Hugh McKinstry was my mentor at Harvard Graduate School of Arts and Sciences. He guided me through my Ph.D. studies, especially my research on manganese oxide as an indicator for ore deposits in the Tintic District. His expertise and support were crucial during my academic development.

My Ph.D. thesis at Harvard focused on the Tintic Mining District in Utah, where I studied manganese oxide as a guide to ore deposits. This area was significant for my early research and helped me understand ore geology in a practical mining context. The district’s geology was a key part of my academic foundation.

From 1960 to 1967, I worked as an exploration geologist for Kennecott Copper Corporation. During this period, I developed new procedures for copper exploration and researched leached cappings, traveling extensively in search of deposits. This role was my first major professional experience in the mining industry.

I served as Vice President of Exploration at Occidental Minerals, a division of Occidental Petroleum, from 1967 to 1975. I worked closely with Paul Bailly and Armand Hammer, and was involved with the Gore family during this dynamic period. It was a time full of challenges and opportunities in mineral exploration.

During my time at Occidental Minerals, I worked closely with Paul Bailly. Together, we navigated the complexities of exploration projects under the leadership of Armand Hammer and the Gore family. Paul was a key collaborator in that period of my career.

Armand Hammer was a prominent figure at Occidental Petroleum while I was Vice President of Exploration there from 1967 to 1975. I worked alongside him and others like Paul Bailly and the Gore family during a dynamic time in the company’s history. His leadership influenced many of our exploration initiatives.

The Gore family was involved with Occidental Petroleum during my tenure as Vice President of Exploration from 1967 to 1975. I worked with them alongside Armand Hammer and Paul Bailly during a period full of exploration challenges and opportunities. Their involvement was significant in company affairs.

I served as Vice President of Exploration at Homestake Mining Company from 1975 to 1987. We were eager to discover a major gold deposit and made advances in exploration techniques, including mineral zoning and classifying gold deposits worldwide. I also researched mercury hot springs and studied old Homestake files to support our efforts.

The Manhattan Mine was part of a project I was involved with alongside the McLaughlin Mine and the One Shot Mining Company. We worked to reassure Bill Wilder about his rights and managed issues related to the Gamble Ranch. Environmental concerns were significant, and we engaged with the community to present Homestake positively despite opposition.

My experience at the McLaughlin Mine began as a metallurgical technician and mill operator, where I learned about safety and operations such as running crushers and autoclaves. I was promoted to assistant foreman and later process specialist. The work was demanding but rewarding, and I valued the training and leadership I received there.

The One Shot Mining Company was involved in the Manhattan/McLaughlin Mine project, where we worked closely with Bill Wilder. We focused on protecting his rights and managing the Gamble Ranch property. The company was part of a complex project that required balancing mining operations with environmental and community concerns.

Bill Wilder was a key figure in the Manhattan/McLaughlin Mine project, and we worked to reassure him about his rights during the development process. His involvement was important as we navigated environmental concerns and community opposition. We also coordinated efforts related to the Gamble Ranch property.

The Gamble Ranch was a significant property involved in the Manhattan/McLaughlin Mine project. We worked on managing this land alongside the One Shot Mining Company and Bill Wilder. Environmental issues were a major focus as we presented Homestake to the local community and addressed opposition.

From 1987 to 1995, I was CEO and Chief Environmental Officer of Minven. During this time, I also served on the California Mining and Geology Board and worked on issues like the California Desert Conservation Area and the Summitville mine disaster. I emphasized the importance of responsible mining, including sand and gravel extraction.

I was involved with the California Mining and Geology Board while serving as CEO of Minven from 1987 to 1995. The board work included addressing environmental issues such as the California Desert Conservation Area and the Summitville mine disaster. This role allowed me to influence mining policies and environmental protections in California.

While CEO of Minven and a member of the California Mining and Geology Board, I worked on issues related to the California Desert Conservation Area. This involved balancing mining interests with environmental conservation efforts. It was part of a broader commitment to responsible resource management in California.

The Summitville mine disaster was a significant environmental event I dealt with during my tenure as CEO of Minven and my involvement with the California Mining and Geology Board. It underscored the importance of environmental oversight in mining operations. I worked to promote safer and cleaner mining practices in response.

I have been active in community activism in the Capay Valley, working to preserve the environment and oppose projects like the Davis Creek Dam. Our efforts included blocking approval of environmental impact reports and raising concerns about air and water pollution. It has been a long-running and challenging fight to protect this area.

The Davis Creek Dam project was one I actively opposed as part of my environmental work in the Capay Valley. We worked to block its approval by challenging environmental impact reports and highlighting pollution concerns. This was part of a broader effort to protect local ecosystems and water quality.

In 1981, the Solano Irrigation District became involved with the McLaughlin Mine due to concerns about water quality in the Berryessa watershed. Betsy Marchand alerted us to potential impacts, and we pushed for strict oversight including fail-safe tailings containment and daily monitoring. Our vigilance helped protect Solano County’s water resources.

Betsy Marchand played a crucial role in alerting the Solano Irrigation District and others about the McLaughlin Mine’s potential impact on water quality in the Berryessa watershed. Her warnings in 1981 helped galvanize efforts to ensure environmental safeguards were put in place. She was an important advocate for protecting local water resources.

The Berryessa watershed was a key environmental concern during the McLaughlin Mine operations. We worked closely with the Solano Irrigation District and community advocates like Betsy Marchand to protect its water quality. Our efforts included legal threats and demanding strict monitoring and containment measures to prevent pollution.

Solano County became a focal point for environmental protection related to the McLaughlin Mine. We ensured the county was involved in planning and oversight to safeguard its water resources. Through cooperation with water law specialists and rigorous monitoring, we made the mine one of the cleanest operations with no threat to county waters.
